context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 1


Problem 1.1 (20 pts)

For the mass and spring discussed during the lecture (Howard Georgi Eq. (1.1)–(1.8)), suppose that the system is hung vertically in the earth's gravitational field, with the top of the spring held fixed. Show that the frequency for vertical oscillations is given by Eq. (1.5). Explain why gravity has no effect on the angular frequency.


Problem 1.2 (20 pts)

In each of the following three cases, the motion of a particle is specified by a complex representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find a real representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    η(t) = c cos ωt + d sin ωt
where c and d are real, time-independent quantities. A, B, C, and τ are real quantities. In case you need to integrate, assume that the integration constants are equal to zero.

(a) X(t) = A·e^(−i(ωt − π/2))
(b) Ẋ(t) = Biωτ·e^(−i(ωt + π/6))
(c) Ẍ(t) = C·(1/(1 + iωτ))·e^(−iωt)

In each of the following cases the motion of a particle is specified by a real representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find the complex representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    Q(t) = Q·e^(−i(ωt + φ))
where Q and φ are real, time-independent quantities. α, β, γ, and δ are real and positive quantities.

(d) x(t) = α cos ωt − β sin ωt
(e) ẋ(t) = −γ sin(ωt + π/3)
(f) ẍ(t) = δ cos(ωt + π/6)


Problem 1.3 (20 pts)

A block of mass M slides without friction between two springs of spring constants K and 2K as shown in Figure 1. The system is constrained to move only along the axis of the springs.

[Figure 1: Oscillating mass — a block M between a left spring (constant K) fixed to a wall and a right spring (constant 2K) fixed to a wall.]

(a) Calculate the angular frequency of oscillations.
(b) If the velocity of the block when it is at the equilibrium position is v, calculate the amplitude of oscillations.
(c) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a real function, x(t) (with the initial condition from (b)).
(d) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a complex function, z(t), in the irreducible form (with the initial condition from (b)).


Problem 1.4 (20 pts)

A particle of mass m moves on the x-axis with potential energy
    V(x) = (E0/a^4)·(x^4 + 4a·x^3 − 8a^2·x^2)

(a) Find the positions at which the particle is in stable equilibrium.
(b) Find the angular frequency of small oscillations about each stable equilibrium position.
(c) What do you mean by small oscillations? Be quantitative and give a separate answer for each point of stable equilibrium.


Problem 1.5 (20 pts)

Consider a simple pendulum consisting of a point-like mass m attached to a massless string of length L hanging from a fixed support and constrained to move in a vertical plane (see Figure 2). Assume gravitational acceleration to be g.

[Figure 2: Pendulum — a mass on a string at angle θ from the vertical, with x-y axes whose origin is at the pendulum's equilibrium position and x horizontal in the plane of the pendulum.]

(a) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the angle θ, its deviation from the vertical. Find the exact equation of motion (τ = Iα) for the pendulum as a function of θ.
(b) Assume that the angle θ is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(c) Justify your approximations. Find the range of θ for which the pendulum can be considered a SHM. What is the period of oscillations of this SHM?
(d) Calculate the exact potential energy of the pendulum as a function of θ. Then, show that the Taylor expansion leads to the same result as in part (b).
(e) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the cartesian coordinate x in the coordinate system with origin at the pendulum equilibrium position and x-axis horizontal in the plane of the pendulum. Find the exact equation of motion (F = ma) of the pendulum in terms of x.
(f) Assume that x is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(g) Justify your approximations. Find the range of x such that the pendulum can be considered a SHM.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Uh I’m kind of lost on Problem Set 1 already. It all feels like the same oscillation stuff but written in different ways, and I can’t tell where to even start.
exchanges.tutor: That feeling is totally normal at the start — the problem set does cover a lot of ground. Let's narrow it down: which specific problem or part of a problem are you looking at right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed. I should acknowledge their feeling briefly, then use guided discovery to help them identify a starting point without giving away any solutions. I'll ask a focusing question to help them orient themselves to the problem set rather than diving into any specific problem yet. I want to keep it bite-sized and let them lead me to which problem they want to tackle first.

exchanges.student: I was staring at 1.2, but then 1.1 also confused me because gravity seems like it should change the spring motion. I guess I don’t really know the “big picture” for how all these problems connect.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to start with 1.1 — it's foundational. Let me ask you this: when a mass on a vertical spring reaches its new equilibrium (with gravity), what forces are acting on it, and how do they balance?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is jumping between problems and looking for a big picture connection. I should help them focus on one problem at a time rather than trying to solve everything at once. Problem 1.1 seems like a natural starting point since it's foundational and the student already expressed a genuine conceptual confusion about it (why doesn't gravity affect frequency?). I'll ask a Socratic question to get them thinking about the physics of the spring-mass system with gravity, without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I think spring force and gravity, but then I get stuck because if gravity is always there, I don’t see why it wouldn’t affect the frequency too. Also I’m not sure if I’m supposed to measure position from the unstretched spring or from the new equilibrium, and that keeps messing me up.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually right on the edge of the key insight. Try this: write out Newton's second law for the mass when it's displaced a small distance x from the *new* equilibrium position. What does that equation look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the key tension correctly - they understand the forces but are confused about the reference point. The choice of coordinate is exactly the key insight for problem 1.1. I should ask a Socratic question that guides them to see what happens to the equation of motion when they measure displacement from the new equilibrium position, without telling them the answer.

exchanges.student: That’s where I blank out — if it’s from the new equilibrium, I feel like I’m secretly “getting rid of” gravity and I don’t know why that’s allowed. Could you just explain the whole setup cleanly, including why the reference point matters?
exchanges.tutor: I won't walk through the whole setup for you — that's yours to work out. But here's a targeted question: first, write the force balance equation *at* the new equilibrium (when the mass is stationary). Then write Newton's second law when the mass is displaced by x from that point. What do you notice when you subtract one from the other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole setup, which would violate the 'never give the answer directly' rule. However, their confusion is genuine and specific - they feel uncomfortable about 'getting rid of' gravity. I should push back on the request for a full explanation and instead ask a targeted Socratic question that helps them see why choosing the equilibrium point is mathematically legitimate. I'll ask them to write the force balance at equilibrium first, then at a displaced position, so they can see the cancellation themselves.

exchanges.student: I think the equilibrium one is something like \(k\Delta L = mg\), but then after that I start mixing up \(\Delta L\) and \(x\). And now I’m also wondering if this same trick is supposed to work for the pendulum small-angle thing and the weird potential in 1.4, because it all kind of blurs together for me.
exchanges.tutor: Good — k·ΔL = mg is exactly right for equilibrium. Now: ΔL is the *fixed* stretch at equilibrium, and x is any *additional* displacement from there. So when the mass moves by x from equilibrium, what is the total spring stretch in terms of ΔL and x? Write out Newton's second law with that total stretch.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the equilibrium condition, which is great progress. They're mixing up ΔL and x, which is a common confusion. I should affirm their equilibrium equation and help them clarify the distinction between ΔL (static stretch at equilibrium) and x (additional displacement from equilibrium). Their question about whether this connects to 1.4 and 1.5 is interesting but is a detour - I should briefly acknowledge the connection but redirect them to finish 1.1 first. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: So maybe total stretch is \(\Delta L + x\), and then force is \(k(\Delta L + x)\), but I’m not confident about the sign at all. I’m still kind of hoping for the general idea across these problems, because I keep losing track of what’s equilibrium shift versus what actually changes the oscillation.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right stretch expression. On signs: gravity pulls down, the spring pulls up. If downward is positive, write the full Newton's second law with those two forces. What do you get, and what happens when you substitute in k·ΔL = mg?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right expression for total stretch. They're unsure about signs, which is important but manageable. I should affirm their progress and help them think about signs by asking about direction of forces. Their request for a 'general idea across problems' is a recurring detour - I should briefly but firmly redirect them to finish 1.1 first. We're close to the key insight and I don't want them to lose momentum.

exchanges.student: I think I’d get something like \(m\ddot x = mg - k(\Delta L + x)\), and then maybe the \(mg\) and \(k\Delta L\) cancel so it becomes \(m\ddot x = -kx\)? If that’s right, I still don’t totally get the broader pattern, like when a constant force just shifts equilibrium versus when it changes the frequency.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that cancellation is the whole point. Now, looking at m·ẍ = -kx, what does that tell you about the angular frequency? And what does the absence of g in that equation tell you about gravity's role? Once you can answer those, you'll be ready to write up your solution to 1.1 and move to the next problem.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully derived the key result for 1.1 — this is excellent progress and I should acknowledge it clearly. Their question about the broader pattern is now more legitimate since they've earned it through their work, but I should be careful not to give a lecture. I'll affirm their result, ask them to articulate what it tells them about frequency, and briefly note that the 'broader pattern' question is best answered by working through the other problems — letting them discover it inductively rather than me explaining it deductively.

exchanges.student: So I guess \(\omega=\sqrt{k/m}\), same as horizontal, and gravity just changes the equilibrium position. I’m still shaky because that seems almost too convenient, and I’m not sure how to tell in other problems whether something only shifts equilibrium or also changes the restoring-force slope.
exchanges.tutor: You've got it exactly right — and your instinct to ask 'when does this pattern hold?' is a good one. But the honest answer is: you'll see it more clearly by *doing* 1.4 and 1.5 than by me describing it abstractly. Can you write up your full solution to 1.1 now, then we'll move on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified ω = √(k/m) and the key insight about gravity only shifting equilibrium. Their lingering doubt is healthy scientific skepticism but the 'broader pattern' question is becoming repetitive - this is the third time they've asked about it. Rather than answering that abstractly, I should push them to consolidate what they've learned by writing up 1.1, and point out that the answer to their broader question will emerge naturally from doing 1.4 and 1.5. This is a good moment to ask them to write their solution attempt for 1.1 and move on.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I’m worried I’d just parrot the cancellation without really understanding it. Could I switch to 1.4 for a second? That one looks totally different, and I think seeing how equilibrium and frequency come out of a potential might make the pattern click for me.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but write up 1.1 first — even a rough attempt. You've done the hard thinking already; writing it out is part of the learning, not just a formality. Give it a shot and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to jump to 1.4 before writing up 1.1. Their reasoning has some merit - 1.4 does involve finding equilibrium from a potential, which is a different approach. However, they haven't consolidated 1.1 yet, and jumping around risks losing the progress made. I should be firm about writing up 1.1 first - it doesn't have to be perfect, but they need to practice articulating what they've worked out. Keep it brief and direct.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly not sure how detailed it should be, and when I try to write it I start second-guessing every step. Maybe I should just understand the general method first, because right now 1.1, 1.4, and 1.5 all feel like the same idea in different disguises and I can’t separate them.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually been going in circles on this 'general pattern' question for a few exchanges now. You already have everything you need for 1.1 — the equilibrium condition, the displaced equation, and the cancellation. The pattern you're looking for *emerges from doing the problems*, not from understanding it abstractly first. Write up 1.1, even imperfectly. That's the next step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles - they've now asked about the 'general pattern' or 'broader method' multiple times and are avoiding writing up 1.1 despite having all the pieces. This is a redundancy pattern I should directly address. I need to be firm and direct: they have everything they need for 1.1, the best way to understand the pattern is to write concrete solutions, and I won't engage with the abstract 'general method' request. I should be encouraging but clear that writing is the next step.

deductions.reason: After the student correctly derives m·ẍ = -kx and states ω = √(k/m) in Turn 7, the tutor continues to cycle on the same 'general pattern' theme across Turns 8–10 without consolidating and moving forward. The student repeatedly raises the same concern about the broader pattern, and the tutor's responses (while reasonable) do not reframe or synthesize in a way that breaks the loop — the conversation ends with the student still circling the same point without meaningful progression.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic technique throughout, consistently refusing to give away answers and instead prompting the student with targeted sub-questions. The guided discovery for Problem 1.1 was well-executed: the tutor led the student step-by-step to derive the cancellation of gravity without doing the work for them. Scaffolding was solid — the tutor built on each correct student response. The one notable weakness is that after the student had essentially solved 1.1 (Turn 7), the conversation spiraled on the 'general pattern' question for three more turns without meaningful synthesis or reframing, triggering the redundancy criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Pedagogy (1.1–1.3): The tutor never gave submission-ready answers, never rewrote student text, and consistently let the student generate key ideas through guided questioning. The student arrived at m·ẍ = -kx and ω = √(k/m) through their own reasoning. No deductions apply. Dialogue quality (2.1): After Turn 7, the student had the correct answer for 1.1 but kept raising the same 'general pattern' concern. The tutor repeated the same directive ('write up 1.1') across Turns 8, 9, and 10 without switching to a different strategy — such as briefly addressing the pattern question, offering a concrete comparison to 1.4, or summarizing the insight more explicitly to break the loop. This constitutes pattern-level repetition without synthesis, warranting the full 4-point deduction. Dialogue quality (2.2): The tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment throughout. Communication quality (3.1–3.2): Responses were appropriately concise and interactive, tone was supportive and non-evaluative.
